lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans greetings change with the time of day: "Goeie môre" (good morning), "Goeie middag" (good afternoon), "Goeie naand" (good evening). "Hoe gaan dit?" literally means "How goes it?".

lessons.title: Yes, No & Politeness
lessons.culturalNote: "Asseblief" (please) and "dankie" (thank you) are used everywhere. "Jammer" means "sorry". Adding "asseblief" to any request makes it polite.

lessons.title: Family
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans family words are warm and short. Children often call their parents "Ma" and "Pa". Grandparents are "Ouma" and "Oupa".

exercises.meaning: My child is five years old.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–10
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans numbers are close to Dutch and German. Notice "sewe" (seven) and "agt" (eight).

lessons.title: Food & Drink
lessons.culturalNote: South African food culture is rich in Afrikaans words: "braai" (barbecue), "biltong" (dried meat), and "koeksister" (a sweet treat). Here are the basics.

lessons.title: Directions
lessons.culturalNote: Start a request for directions with "Verskoon my" (excuse me). "Regs" is right and "links" is left — easy to remember as left = links.

lessons.title: Introducing Yourself
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans is warm and direct. 'My naam is...' means 'My name is...'. 'Aangename kennis' is a polite 'Nice to meet you'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “What is your name?”?

lessons.title: At the Shop
lessons.culturalNote: Shopping phrases you'll use daily. 'Hoeveel kos dit?' asks the price; 'Asseblief' (please) is everywhere.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–10
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans numbers are close to Dutch and English roots — 'twee' (two), 'drie' (three), 'tien' (ten).

lessons.title: Food & Drink
lessons.culturalNote: Food brings people together. Offer a guest 'koffie' (coffee) and 'brood' (bread) — it's a sign of hospitality.

lessons.title: Days of the Week
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans day names are close to Dutch and German. The week runs Maandag (Monday) to Sondag (Sunday).

lessons.title: Colours
lessons.culturalNote: Colours in Afrikaans are short and handy: 'rooi' (red), 'blou' (blue), 'groen' (green). They come before the noun, like English.

lessons.title: Feelings
lessons.culturalNote: Tell people how you feel: 'Ek is bly' (I'm happy), 'Ek is moeg' (I'm tired), 'Ek is honger' (I'm hungry).

lessons.title: The Body
lessons.culturalNote: Body words are useful at the doctor: 'My kop is seer' (My head is sore). 'Oë' (eyes) and 'oor' (ear) are common ones to know.

lessons.title: Time & Routine
lessons.culturalNote: 'Môre' means both 'tomorrow' and 'morning' — context tells them apart. 'Vandag' (today) and 'gister' (yesterday) complete the set.

lessons.title: Animals
lessons.culturalNote: Afrikaans animal names are short and fun: 'hond' (dog), 'kat' (cat), 'voël' (bird).

lessons.title: Around Town
lessons.culturalNote: Useful places to name: 'skool' (school), 'hospitaal' (hospital), 'winkel' (shop). Ask 'Waar is...?' (Where is...?).

lessons.title: Useful Verbs
lessons.culturalNote: A handful of verbs unlocks real sentences: 'Ek wil eet' (I want to eat), 'Ek gaan' (I go).

lessons.title: In the Home
lessons.culturalNote: Name the rooms and furniture: 'deur' (door), 'venster' (window), 'kombuis' (kitchen). Handy for describing where things are.

lessons.title: Clothing
lessons.culturalNote: Clothes are short and handy in Afrikaans: 'hemp' (shirt), 'broek' (trousers), 'skoene' (shoes).

lessons.title: Nature & Places
lessons.culturalNote: The Cape has it all: 'berg' (mountain), 'see' (sea), 'rivier' (river). 'Dorp' (town) and 'plaas' (farm) describe where people live.

lessons.title: Question Words
lessons.culturalNote: The question words: 'wie' (who), 'wat' (what), 'waar' (where), 'hoekom' (why). Pair them with verbs to ask anything.

lessons.title: Action Verbs
lessons.culturalNote: Core verbs unlock sentences: 'Ek hou van...' (I like...), 'Ek wil...' (I want...), 'Ek weet' (I know).

lessons.title: Fruit & Veg
lessons.culturalNote: Fresh from the mark: 'piesang' (banana), 'lemoen' (orange), 'aartappel' (potato).

lessons.title: In the Kitchen
lessons.culturalNote: Set the table: 'bord' (plate), 'koppie' (cup), 'mes' (knife), 'vurk' (fork), 'lepel' (spoon).

lessons.title: Bigger Numbers
lessons.culturalNote: Counting higher: 'elf' (eleven), 'twintig' (twenty), 'honderd' (hundred), 'duisend' (thousand).

lessons.title: Transport
lessons.culturalNote: Ways to travel: 'motor' (car), 'bus' (bus), 'trein' (train), 'fiets' (bicycle).

lessons.title: People & Family
lessons.culturalNote: The wider family: 'vriend' (friend), 'oom' (uncle), 'tante' (aunt). 'Man' is husband/man; 'vrou' is wife/woman.

lessons.title: Sports & Fun
lessons.culturalNote: Time to play: 'bal' (ball), 'speel' (to play), 'sing' (to sing), 'dans' (to dance), 'musiek' (music).

lessons.title: Helpful Phrases
lessons.culturalNote: Handy phrases: 'Jammer' (sorry), 'Ek verstaan nie' (I don't understand), 'Praat jy Engels?' (Do you speak English?).

reading.title: Hallo, Pieter!
reading.intro: Two friends say hello. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.t: Hallo Pieter. My naam is Anja.
lines.en: Hello Pieter. My name is Anja.
Good, thank you. And with you?

reading.title: Goeie môre!
reading.intro: A friendly morning greeting. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.t: Goeie môre! My naam is Pieter.
lines.en: Good morning! My name is Pieter.

lines.t: Goeie môre, Pieter. Ek is Lerato.
lines.en: Good morning, Pieter. I am Lerato.
